skill.name: ai-code-cleanup
skill.description: Find and remove common AI-generated code artifacts such as redundant comments, defensive bloat, type workarounds, and style drift.
skill.summary: Clean AI-assisted code changes while preserving behavior and project style.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backticks, placeholder words, and Markdown code fences as high-risk patterns. Review found no prompt injection, malware intent, credential access, network exfiltration, or automatic command execution. The main remaining risk is semantic: the skill can guide agents to remove defensive checks, so users should verify trust boundaries and run tests.

medium_findings.title: Security Regression Risk From Removing Defensive Checks
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends removing redundant validation and defensive null checks when callers already validate inputs. This can be useful, but a wrong trust-boundary judgment could weaken production code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance explicitly targets defensive code removal, but also tells users to keep defensive code for untrusted inputs. The risk depends on correct project context and test coverage.

low_findings.title: Local Git Command Example
low_findings.description: The concrete shell command is a local git diff example for listing changed files. It has no network access, secret reads, destructive flags, or evidence of automatic execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is a standard repository inspection command in a documentation block. It uses command substitution, but only to compute a merge base for git diff.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Test Command Block
low_findings.description: The reference template contains a bash fenced block with a user-filled test command placeholder. It is documentation, not an executable script bundled with the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is real Markdown shell syntax, but the content is a placeholder. Any risk would come from later user-supplied commands, not from this skill file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick and Code Fence Matches
low_findings.description: Most external-command alerts are Markdown prompts, inline file references, code samples, or template fences. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or automatic command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are in Markdown documentation and examples. They do not define executable code paths, package scripts, hooks, or runtime files.

low_findings.title: Sensitive and Cryptography Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak-cryptography alerts are substring matches inside placeholders or prose, such as sample code placeholders and description text. No credential store or cryptographic algorithm usage was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms appear as parts of ordinary words or placeholder names. There is no file access to Windows SAM, and no MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4 implementation.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access False Positive in Regex Example
low_findings.description: The filesystem alerts point to dot characters inside an email-regex example. The lines do not access dotfiles, hidden directories, or filesystem APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are inside a before-and-after code sample for comment cleanup. The dot is part of a regular expression, not a path.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Keyword Collision
low_findings.description: The system-reconnaissance alert points to an example type check on a userId value. No operating-system probing or environment discovery was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is contained in an illustrative JavaScript function and checks application input type. It does not run host reconnaissance commands or read system metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents a git command that uses shell command substitution. It is legitimate local repository analysis, but users should review commands before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command-substitution syntax is present and could be risky in other contexts. Here it is a static git example with no untrusted data source shown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Placeholder Shell Commands in Template
dangerous_patterns.description: The refactoring template reserves space for verification commands. It does not ship executable commands, but generated plans should not add destructive commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risk is contextual because the template expects future user-provided commands. The current file contains only a placeholder.

content.value_statement: AI-assisted coding can leave redundant comments, defensive bloat, type workarounds, and style drift. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through focused cleanup while preserving behavior and tests.
actual_capabilities: Identifies redundant comments that restate obvious code.
Finds unnecessary defensive checks on trusted paths.
Flags type casts and TypeScript suppressions that hide weak typing.
Standardizes naming, formatting, imports, and variable declarations against local style.
Uses git diff context to focus cleanup on changed branch files.
Encourages compilation and test verification after edits.
limitations: It cannot prove behavior is unchanged without project tests or review.
It may misjudge whether validation is redundant at trust boundaries.
It does not provide a full security audit of the codebase.
It depends on existing project conventions being clear enough to follow.

use_cases.title: Prepare AI-Assisted Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Clean branch diffs before review by removing artifacts that make code harder to scan.

use_cases.title: Reduce Review Noise
use_cases.description: Focus review on behavior changes by removing verbose comments, extra guards, and inconsistent style.

use_cases.title: Standardize Generated Code
use_cases.description: Align AI-produced changes with local naming, typing, and error-handling practices.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Current Branch
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner cleanup after an AI coding session
prompt_templates.prompt: Clean up AI-generated artifacts in the current branch. Preserve behavior, follow project style, and run the relevant tests.

prompt_templates.title: Review Changed Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the changed files in this branch for redundant comments, defensive bloat, type workarounds, and style drift. Make minimal edits only.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Defensive Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Find defensive checks added by AI. Remove only checks that are redundant after verifying callers, trust boundaries, and existing tests.

prompt_templates.title: Create Refactoring Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cleanup plan for this component. Include code smells, target state, verification steps, risks, and rollback notes before editing.

output_examples.input: A pull request contains verbose comments, extra null checks, and inconsistent variable names after AI assistance.
output: A concise cleanup summary grouped by file.
A list of removed redundant comments and unnecessary guards.
A verification note showing which tests or checks passed.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript change includes broad casts and suppressions added to satisfy the compiler quickly.
output: A focused review of type workarounds that should be removed or replaced.
Cleaner typing suggestions that preserve behavior.
A short list of items needing human judgment.

output_examples.input: A component needs cleanup before a larger refactor.
output: A refactoring plan with current problems, target state, steps, risks, and verification strategy.
A rollback note for each meaningful change phase.

best_practices: Run the cleanup on a small branch and review every behavior-affecting edit.
Keep defensive code when inputs cross a trust boundary or caller guarantees are unclear.
Run tests, type checks, and formatters after each meaningful cleanup step.
anti_patterns: Do not remove validation only because it looks verbose.
Do not replace project conventions with a generic style preference.
Do not make broad refactors while performing AI artifact cleanup.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically delete code?
faq.answer: No. It guides an AI assistant to inspect changed files and make focused cleanup edits with verification.

faq.question: Is this a security auditing skill?
faq.answer: No. It improves code quality after AI-assisted development, but it does not replace a security review.

faq.question: Can it remove important validation?
faq.answer: Yes, if used carelessly. Validate trust boundaries and keep checks for untrusted inputs.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: The guidance is language-neutral, with examples in JavaScript and TypeScript.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it after AI-assisted coding and before code review or branch merge.

faq.question: Does it require project tests?
faq.answer: Tests are strongly recommended because the skill depends on verification to preserve behavior.
